domain: a small-business owner's life: the coffee shop, employees, suppliers, a renovation, family, and a side venture

turns.text: Big news, I finally signed the lease and opened my coffee shop, Maple & Crow, on Birch Street this morning. My very first hire is a barista named Marcus.
Congratulations on opening Maple & Crow on Birch Street! Bringing Marcus on as your first barista is a great start.
Sorted out my beans. I'm buying espresso from a roaster called Cedar Peak, and our house blend is their Midnight Harbor roast.
Cedar Peak as your roaster with the Midnight Harbor blend sounds like a solid foundation for the menu.
Random aside, my sister Tara is getting married in the fall and asked me to be in the wedding party. I'm thrilled but nervous about the toast.
That's wonderful news about Tara's wedding. A toast is daunting, but you'll do great.
Yeah, I have months to prepare so I'm not panicking yet.
Plenty of time. Jot down a few stories now while they're fresh.
Marcus has really stepped up, so I made him my shift lead this week. He basically runs the floor when I'm not there.
Promoting Marcus to shift lead makes sense given how much he's handling. That should free you up considerably.
I've decided to gut and renovate the back room to add eight more seats. The contractor I hired is a firm called Holloway Build.
Expanding seating with the back room makes sense as you grow. Holloway Build handling it should keep things on track.
My espresso machine is a temperamental old La Marzocco. I named her Gertrude because she hisses when she's unhappy.
Gertrude the La Marzocco it is. Old machines have personality, for better or worse.
Hired a second barista today, a college kid named Devon who works weekends. Good energy, learns fast.
Devon sounds like a great weekend addition to the team. Quick learners are worth a lot behind the bar.
Spent the evening on my taxes. I switched from spreadsheets to QuickBooks this year and my accountant is a woman named Renata.
Moving to QuickBooks should make next year smoother, and having Renata on the books helps.
Got chatting with Marcus during close. Turns out he grew up in Tucson and only moved out here for school a few years back.
Tucson to here is quite a change. Nice to learn a bit more about his background.
Cedar Peak is raising prices again. Their rep mentioned they source most of their beans from a single farm in Guatemala called Finca Aurora.
Finca Aurora in Guatemala being their main origin explains a lot about the flavor profile. Price hikes are tough, though.
The renovation hit a snag. Holloway Build found old knob-and-tube wiring behind the wall, so we're delayed two weeks for an electrical permit.
Old wiring is a classic surprise in older buildings. A two-week delay for the permit is frustrating but better safe than sorry.
I'm starting a little side venture: a monthly subscription box of single-origin beans shipped to people's homes. I'm calling it Crow's Nest Club.
Crow's Nest Club is a clever name and a natural extension of the shop. A subscription box could be a nice recurring revenue stream.
Devon surprised me, he's actually a graphic design major. He volunteered to do all the visual work for Crow's Nest Club, the labels and the box artwork.
That's a fantastic bit of luck. Having Devon handle the visuals for Crow's Nest Club in-house will save you a fortune and keep the branding consistent.
Quiet weekend. I finally repotted all the plants on my apartment windowsill and binged an old detective series. Recharged.
Sounds like exactly the kind of low-key weekend that resets you. Repotting is oddly satisfying.
The back room is finally done and we held a little reopening. To handle the extra tables I bumped Marcus up again, he's now my assistant manager and gets a cut of the tips pool.
Assistant manager is a well-earned step up for Marcus, and the back room being finished is a big milestone. Congrats on the reopening.
Renata called with my Q1 numbers. The subscription side is doing better than the cafe margins, honestly. She suggested I incorporate the side venture separately as an LLC.
Interesting that the subscription is outpacing the cafe on margins. Renata's advice to spin it into its own LLC is worth taking seriously.
Devon graduates this month and is moving to Portland for a full-time design job. Bittersweet, but I'm proud of him.
That's a great opportunity for Devon, even if it leaves a gap on your weekend crew. Portland's lucky to have him.

queries.question: Which city did my shift lead Marcus originally come from?
What is the current job title I gave Marcus, my first barista?
Which country is connected to my roaster, Cedar Peak?
Who took charge of designing the look of my Crow's Nest Club subscription?
What unexpected issue did my contractor Holloway Build run into on the job?
Which US city is my barista Devon relocating to?
What is the name of my coffee shop and what street is it on?
What did I name my old La Marzocco espresso machine?
What accounting software did I switch to this year?
What is the name of my monthly bean subscription box side venture?
